Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the substitution $$( b + x )^{r} = ( b + a )^{r} ( 1 + \frac{x - a}{b + a} )^{r}$$ in the binomial expansion to find the Taylor series of each function with the given center.
Question: $$\sqrt{x + 2}$$ at $$a = 0$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the substitution $$( b + x )^{r} = ( b + a )^{r} ( 1 + \frac{x - a}{b + a} )^{r}$$ in the binomial expansion to find the Taylor series of each function with the given center.
Question: $$\sqrt{x^{2} + 2}$$ at $$a = 0$$
Answer: $$\sqrt{2 + x^{2}} = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} 2^{( 1 \text{/} 2 ) - n} \left (\begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2} \\ n\end{matrix}\right ) x^{2 n} ; ( | x^{2} | < 2 )$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the substitution $$( b + x )^{r} = ( b + a )^{r} ( 1 + \frac{x - a}{b + a} )^{r}$$ in the binomial expansion to find the Taylor series of each function with the given center.
Question: $$\sqrt{x + 2}$$ at $$a = 1$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the substitution $$( b + x )^{r} = ( b + a )^{r} ( 1 + \frac{x - a}{b + a} )^{r}$$ in the binomial expansion to find the Taylor series of each function with the given center.
Question: $$\sqrt{2 x - x^{2}}$$ at $$a = 1$$ ( Hint: $$2 x - x^{2} = 1 - ( x - 1 )^{2} )$$
Answer: $$\sqrt{2 x - x^{2}} = \sqrt{1 - ( x - 1 )^{2}}$$ so $$\sqrt{2 x - x^{2}} = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} ( -1 )^{n} \left (\begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2} \\ n\end{matrix}\right ) ( x - 1 )^{2 n}$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the substitution $$( b + x )^{r} = ( b + a )^{r} ( 1 + \frac{x - a}{b + a} )^{r}$$ in the binomial expansion to find the Taylor series of each function with the given center.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the binomial theorem to estimate each number, computing enough terms to obtain an estimate accurate to an error of at most $$1 \text{/} 1000 .$$
Question: [T] $$( 15 )^{1 \text{/} 4}$$ using $$( 16 - x )^{1 \text{/} 4}$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the binomial theorem to estimate each number, computing enough terms to obtain an estimate accurate to an error of at most $$1 \text{/} 1000 .$$
Question: [T] $$( 1001 )^{1 \text{/} 3}$$ using $$( 1000 + x )^{1 \text{/} 3}$$
Answer: $$10 ( 1 + \frac{x}{1000} )^{1 \text{/} 3} = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} 10^{1 - 3 n} \left (\begin{matrix}\frac{1}{3} \\ n\end{matrix}\right ) x^{n} .$$ Using, for example, a fourth-degree estimate at $$x = 1$$ gives $$\left .\begin{matrix}{aa}( 1001 )^{1 \text{/} 3} & \approx 10 ( 1 + \left (\begin{matrix}\frac{1}{3} \\ 1\end{matrix}\right ) 10^{-3} + \left (\begin{matrix}\frac{1}{3} \\ 2\end{matrix}\right ) 10^{-6} + \left (\begin{matrix}\frac{1}{3} \\ 3\end{matrix}\right ) 10^{-9} + \left (\begin{matrix}\frac{1}{3} \\ 4\end{matrix}\right ) 10^{-12} ) \\  & = 10 ( 1 + \frac{1}{3.10^{3}} - \frac{1}{9.10^{6}} + \frac{5}{81.10^{9}} - \frac{10}{243.10^{12}} ) = 10.00333222...\end{matrix}\right .$$ whereas $$( 1001 )^{1 \text{/} 3} = 10.00332222839093 ....$$ Two terms would suffice for three-digit accuracy.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the binomial approximation $$\sqrt{1 - x} \approx 1 - \frac{x}{2} - \frac{x^{2}}{8} - \frac{x^{3}}{16} - \frac{5 x^{4}}{128} - \frac{7 x^{5}}{256}$$ for $$| x | < 1$$ to approximate each number. Compare this value to the value given by a scientific calculator.
Question: [T] $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ using $$x = \frac{1}{2}$$ in $$( 1 - x )^{1 \text{/} 2}$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the binomial approximation $$\sqrt{1 - x} \approx 1 - \frac{x}{2} - \frac{x^{2}}{8} - \frac{x^{3}}{16} - \frac{5 x^{4}}{128} - \frac{7 x^{5}}{256}$$ for $$| x | < 1$$ to approximate each number. Compare this value to the value given by a scientific calculator.
Question: [T] $$\sqrt{5} = 5 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$$ using $$x = \frac{4}{5}$$ in $$( 1 - x )^{1 \text{/} 2}$$
Answer: The approximation is $$2.3152 ;$$ the CAS value is $$2.23 \text{unicode[:ddot.]} .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the binomial approximation $$\sqrt{1 - x} \approx 1 - \frac{x}{2} - \frac{x^{2}}{8} - \frac{x^{3}}{16} - \frac{5 x^{4}}{128} - \frac{7 x^{5}}{256}$$ for $$| x | < 1$$ to approximate each number. Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the binomial approximation $$\sqrt{1 - x} \approx 1 - \frac{x}{2} - \frac{x^{2}}{8} - \frac{x^{3}}{16} - \frac{5 x^{4}}{128} - \frac{7 x^{5}}{256}$$ for $$| x | < 1$$ to approximate each number. Compare this value to the value given by a scientific calculator.
Question: [T] $$\sqrt{6}$$ using $$x = \frac{5}{6}$$ in $$( 1 - x )^{1 \text{/} 2}$$
Answer: The approximation is $$2.583 \text{unicode[:ddot.]} ;$$ the CAS value is $$2.449 \text{unicode[:ddot.]} .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use the binomial approximation $$\sqrt{1 - x} \approx 1 - \frac{x}{2} - \frac{x^{2}}{8} - \frac{x^{3}}{16} - \frac{5 x^{4}}{128} - \frac{7 x^{5}}{256}$$ for $$| x | < 1$$ to approximate each number. Compare this value to the value given by a scientific calculator.
Question: [T] Recall that the graph of $$\sqrt{1 - x^{2}}$$ is an upper semicircle of radius $$1 .$$ Integrate the binomial approximation of $$\sqrt{1 - x^{2}}$$ up to order $$8$$ from $$x = -1$$ to $$x = 1$$ to estimate $$\frac{\pi}{2} .$$

Question: $$F ( x ) = \int_{0}^{x} e^{\text{unicode[:-]} t^{2}} d t ; f ( t ) = e^{\text{unicode[:-]} t^{2}} = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} ( -1 )^{n} \frac{t^{2 n}}{n \text{!}}$$

Question: $$F ( x ) = \int_{0}^{x} \frac{\text{sin} t}{t} d t ; f ( t ) = \frac{\text{sin} t}{t} = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} ( -1 )^{n} \frac{t^{2 n}}{( 2 n + 1 ) \text{!}}$$

Question: $$F ( x ) = \int_{0}^{x} \text{cos} ( \sqrt{t} ) d t ; f ( t ) = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} ( -1 )^{n} \frac{x^{n}}{( 2 n ) \text{!}}$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the Maclaurin series of $$F ( x ) = \int_{0}^{x} f ( t ) d t$$ by integrating the Maclaurin series of $$f$$ term by term. If $$f$$ is not strictly defined at zero, you may substitute the value of the Maclaurin series at zero.
Question: $$F ( x ) = \int_{0}^{x} \frac{1 - \text{cos} t}{t^{2}} d t ; f ( t ) = \frac{1 - \text{cos} t}{t^{2}} = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} ( -1 )^{n} \frac{t^{2 n}}{( 2 n + 2 ) \text{!}}$$

Question: $$F ( x ) = \int_{0}^{x} \frac{\text{ln} ( 1 + t )}{t} d t ; f ( t ) = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} ( -1 )^{n} \frac{t^{n}}{n + 1}$$

Question: $$f ( x ) = \frac{\text{tan} \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}}$$ (see expansion for $$\text{tan} x )$$
Answer: Using the expansion for $$\text{tan} x$$ gives $$1 + \frac{x}{3} + \frac{2 x^{2}}{15} .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: $$\text{ln} ( 1 + x )$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: $$\frac{1}{1 + x^{2}}$$
Answer: $$\frac{1}{1 + x^{2}} = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} ( -1 )^{n} x^{2 n}$$ so $$R = 1$$ by the ratio test.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: $$\text{tan}^{-1} x$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: $$\text{ln} ( 1 + x^{2} )$$
Answer: $$\text{ln} ( 1 + x^{2} ) = \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{( -1 )^{n - 1}}{n} x^{2 n}$$ so $$R = 1$$ by the ratio test.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: Find the Maclaurin series of $$\text{sinh} x = \frac{e^{x} - e^{\text{unicode[:-]} x}}{2} .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: Find the Maclaurin series of $$\text{cosh} x = \frac{e^{x} + e^{\text{unicode[:-]} x}}{2} .$$
Answer: Add series of $$e^{x}$$ and $$e^{\text{unicode[:-]} x}$$ term by term. Odd terms cancel and $$\text{cosh} x = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{2 n}}{( 2 n ) \text{!}} .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: Differentiate term by term the Maclaurin series of $$\text{sinh} x$$ and compare the result with the Maclaurin series of $$\text{cosh} x .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: [T] Let $$S_{n} ( x ) = \sum_{k = 0}^{n} ( -1 )^{k} \frac{x^{2 k + 1}}{( 2 k + 1 ) \text{!}}$$ and $$C_{n} ( x ) = \sum_{n = 0}^{n} ( -1 )^{k} \frac{x^{2 k}}{( 2 k ) \text{!}}$$ denote the respective Maclaurin polynomials of degree $$2 n + 1$$ of $$\text{sin} x$$ and degree $$2 n$$ of $$\text{cos} x .$$ Plot the errors $$\frac{S_{n} ( x )}{C_{n} ( x )} - \text{tan} x$$ for $$n = 1 , .. , 5$$ and compare them to $$x + \frac{x^{3}}{3} + \frac{2 x^{5}}{15} + \frac{17 x^{7}}{315} - \text{tan} x$$ on $$( - \frac{\pi}{4} , \frac{\pi}{4} ) .$$
Answer: The ratio $$\frac{S_{n} ( x )}{C_{n} ( x )}$$ approximates $$\text{tan} x$$ better than does $$p_{7} ( x ) = x + \frac{x^{3}}{3} + \frac{2 x^{5}}{15} + \frac{17 x^{7}}{315}$$ for $$N \geq 3 .$$ The dashed curves are $$\frac{S_{n}}{C_{n}} - \text{tan}$$ for $$n = 1 , 2 .$$ The dotted curve corresponds to $$n = 3 ,$$ and the dash-dotted curve corresponds to $$n = 4 .$$ The solid curve is $$p_{7} - \text{tan} x .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: Use the identity $$2 \text{sin} x \text{cos} x = \text{sin} ( 2 x )$$ to find the power series expansion of $$\text{sin}^{2} x$$ at $$x = 0 .$$ ( Hint: Integrate the Maclaurin series of $$\text{sin} ( 2 x )$$ term by term.)

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: If $$y = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} a_{n} x^{n} ,$$ find the power series expansions of $$x y^{\prime}$$ and $$x^{2} y^{\second} .$$
Answer: By the term-by-term differentiation theorem, $$y^{\prime} = \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} n a_{n} x^{n - 1}$$ so $$y^{\prime} = \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} n a_{n} x^{n - 1} x y^{\prime} = \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} n a_{n} x^{n} ,$$ whereas $$y^{\prime} = \sum_{n = 2}^{\infty} n ( n - 1 ) a_{n} x^{n - 2}$$ so $$x y^{\second} = \sum_{n = 2}^{\infty} n ( n - 1 ) a_{n} x^{n} .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: [T] Suppose that $$y = \sum_{k = 0}^{\infty} a_{k} x^{k}$$ satisfies $$y^{\prime} = -2 x y$$ and $$y ( 0 ) = 0 .$$ Show that $$a_{2 k + 1} = 0$$ for all $$k$$ and that $$a_{2 k + 2} = \frac{\text{unicode[:-]} a_{2 k}}{k + 1} .$$ Plot the partial sum $$S_{20}$$ of $$y$$ on the interval $$[ -4 , 4 ] .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: [T] Suppose that a set of standardized test scores is normally distributed with mean $$\mu = 100$$ and standard deviation $$\sigma = 10 .$$ Set up an integral that represents the probability that a test score will be between $$90$$ and $$110$$ and use the integral of the degree $$10$$ Maclaurin polynomial of $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} e^{\text{unicode[:-]} x^{2} \text{/} 2}$$ to estimate this probability.
Answer: The probability is $$p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} \int_{( a - \mu ) \text{/} \sigma}^{( b - \mu ) \text{/} \sigma} e^{\text{unicode[:-]} x^{2} \text{/} 2} d x$$ where $$a = 90$$ and $$b = 100 ,$$ that is, $$p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} \int_{-1}^{1} e^{\text{unicode[:-]} x^{2} \text{/} 2} d x = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} \int_{-1}^{1} \sum_{n = 0}^{5} ( -1 )^{n} \frac{x^{2 n}}{2^{n} n \text{!}} d x = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} \sum_{n = 0}^{5} ( -1 )^{n} \frac{1}{( 2 n + 1 ) 2^{n} n \text{!}} \approx 0.6827 .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: [T] Suppose that a set of standardized test scores is normally distributed with mean $$\mu = 100$$ and standard deviation $$\sigma = 10 .$$ Set up an integral that represents the probability that a test score will be between $$70$$ and $$130$$ and use the integral of the degree $$50$$ Maclaurin polynomial of $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} e^{\text{unicode[:-]} x^{2} \text{/} 2}$$ to estimate this probability.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: [T] Suppose that $$\sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} a_{n} x^{n}$$ converges to a function $$f ( x )$$ such that $$f ( 0 ) = 1 , f^{\prime} ( 0 ) = 0 ,$$ and $$f^{\second} ( x ) = \text{unicode[:-]} f ( x ) .$$ Find a formula for $$a_{n}$$ and plot the partial sum $$S_{N}$$ for $$N = 20$$ on $$[ -5 , 5 ] .$$
Answer: As in the previous problem one obtains $$a_{n} = 0$$ if $$n$$ is odd and $$a_{n} = \text{unicode[:-]} ( n + 2 ) ( n + 1 ) a_{n + 2}$$ if $$n$$ is even, so $$a_{0} = 1$$ leads to $$a_{2 n} = \frac{( -1 )^{n}}{( 2 n ) \text{!}} .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: [T] Suppose that $$\sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} a_{n} x^{n}$$ converges to a function $$f ( x )$$ such that $$f ( 0 ) = 0 , f^{\prime} ( 0 ) = 1 ,$$ and $$f^{\second} ( x ) = \text{unicode[:-]} f ( x ) .$$ Find a formula for $$a_{n}$$ and plot the partial sum $$S_{N}$$ for $$N = 10$$ on $$[ -5 , 5 ] .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: Suppose that $$\sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} a_{n} x^{n}$$ converges to a function $$y$$ such that $$y^{\second} - y^{\prime} + y = 0$$ where $$y ( 0 ) = 1$$ and $$y \prime ( 0 ) = 0 .$$ Find a formula that relates $$a_{n + 2} , a_{n + 1} ,$$ and $$a_{n}$$ and compute $$a_{0} , ... , a_{5} .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series of each function.
Question: Suppose that $$\sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} a_{n} x^{n}$$ converges to a function $$y$$ such that $$y^{\second} - y^{\prime} + y = 0$$ where $$y ( 0 ) = 0$$ and $$y^{\prime} ( 0 ) = 1 .$$ Find a formula that relates $$a_{n + 2} , a_{n + 1} ,$$ and $$a_{n}$$ and compute $$a_{1} , ... , a_{5} .$$

Problem Statement: The error in approximating the integral $$\int_{a}^{b} f ( t ) d t$$ by that of a Taylor approximation $$\int_{a}^{b} P_{n} ( t ) d t$$ is at most $$\int_{a}^{b} R_{n} ( t ) d t .$$ In the following exercises, the Taylor remainder estimate $$R_{n} \le \frac{M}{( n + 1 ) \text{!}} | x - a |^{n + 1}$$ guarantees that the integral of the Taylor polynomial of the given order approximates the integral of $$f$$ with an error less than $$\frac{1}{10} .$$ a) Evaluate the integral of the appropriate Taylor polynomial and verify that it approximates the CAS value with an error less than $$\frac{1}{100} .$$ b) Compare the accuracy of the polynomial integral estimate with the remainder estimate.
Question: [T] $$\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\text{sin} t}{t} d t ; P_{s} = 1 - \frac{x^{2}}{3 \text{!}} + \frac{x^{4}}{5 \text{!}} - \frac{x^{6}}{7 \text{!}} + \frac{x^{8}}{9 \text{!}}$$ (You may assume that the absolute value of the ninth derivative of $$\frac{\text{sin} t}{t}$$ is bounded by $$0.1 . )$$
Answer: a. (Proof) b. We have $$R_{s} \le \frac{0.1}{( 9 ) \text{!}} \pi^{9} \approx 0.0082 < 0.01 .$$ We have $$\int_{0}^{\pi} ( 1 - \frac{x^{2}}{3 \text{!}} + \frac{x^{4}}{5 \text{!}} - \frac{x^{6}}{7 \text{!}} + \frac{x^{8}}{9 \text{!}} ) d x = \pi - \frac{\pi^{3}}{3 \cdotp 3 \text{!}} + \frac{\pi^{5}}{5 \cdotp 5 \text{!}} - \frac{\pi^{7}}{7 \cdotp 7 \text{!}} + \frac{\pi^{9}}{9 \cdotp 9 \text{!}} = 1.852... ,$$ whereas $$\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\text{sin} t}{t} d t = 1.85194... ,$$ so the actual error is approximately $$0.00006 .$$

Problem Statement: The error in approximating the integral $$\int_{a}^{b} f ( t ) d t$$ by that of a Taylor approximation $$\int_{a}^{b} P_{n} ( t ) d t$$ is at most $$\int_{a}^{b} R_{n} ( t ) d t .$$ In the following exercises, the Taylor remainder estimate $$R_{n} \le \frac{M}{( n + 1 ) \text{!}} | x - a |^{n + 1}$$ guarantees that the integral of the Taylor polynomial of the given order approximates the integral of $$f$$ with an error less than $$\frac{1}{10} .$$ a) Evaluate the integral of the appropriate Taylor polynomial and verify that it approximates the CAS value with an error less than $$\frac{1}{100} .$$ b) Compare the accuracy of the polynomial integral estimate with the remainder estimate.
Question: [T] $$\int_{0}^{2} e^{\text{unicode[:-]} x^{2}} d x ; p_{11} = 1 - x^{2} + \frac{x^{4}}{2} - \frac{x^{6}}{3 \text{!}} + \text{cdots} - \frac{x^{22}}{11 \text{!}}$$ (You may assume that the absolute value of the $$23 \text{rd}$$ derivative of $$e^{\text{unicode[:-]} x^{2}}$$ is less than $$2 \times 10^{14} . )$$

Problem Statement: The following exercises deal with Fresnel integrals .
Question: The Fresnel integrals are defined by $$C ( x ) = \int_{0}^{x} \text{cos} ( t^{2} ) d t$$ and $$S ( x ) = \int_{0}^{x} \text{sin} ( t^{2} ) d t .$$ Compute the power series of $$C ( x )$$ and $$S ( x )$$ and plot the sums $$C_{N} ( x )$$ and $$S_{N} ( x )$$ of the first $$N = 50$$ nonzero terms on $$[ 0 , 2 \pi ] .$$
Answer: Since $$\text{cos} ( t^{2} ) = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} ( -1 )^{n} \frac{t^{4 n}}{( 2 n ) \text{!}}$$ and $$\text{sin} ( t^{2} ) = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} ( -1 )^{n} \frac{t^{4 n + 2}}{( 2 n + 1 ) \text{!}} ,$$ one has $$S ( x ) = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} ( -1 )^{n} \frac{x^{4 n + 3}}{( 4 n + 3 ) ( 2 n + 1 ) \text{!}}$$ and $$C ( x ) = \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} ( -1 )^{n} \frac{x^{4 n + 1}}{( 4 n + 1 ) ( 2 n ) \text{!}} .$$ The sums of the first $$50$$ nonzero terms are plotted below with $$C_{50} ( x )$$ the solid curve and $$S_{50} ( x )$$ the dashed curve.

Problem Statement: The following exercises deal with Fresnel integrals .
Question: [T] The Fresnel integrals are used in design applications for roadways and railways and other applications because of the curvature properties of the curve with coordinates $$( C ( t ) , S ( t ) ) .$$ Plot the curve $$( C_{50} , S_{50} )$$ for $$0 \le t \le 2 \pi ,$$ the coordinates of which were computed in the previous exercise.

Problem Statement: The following exercises deal with Fresnel integrals .
Question: Estimate $$\int_{0}^{1 \text{/} 4} \sqrt{x - x^{2}} d x$$ by approximating $$\sqrt{1 - x}$$ using the binomial approximation $$1 - \frac{x}{2} - \frac{x^{2}}{8} - \frac{x^{3}}{16} - \frac{5 x^{4}}{2128} - \frac{7 x^{5}}{256} .$$

Problem Statement: The following exercises deal with Fresnel integrals .
Question: [T] Use Newton’s approximation of the binomial $$\sqrt{1 - x^{2}}$$ to approximate $$\pi$$ as follows. The circle centered at $$( \frac{1}{2} , 0 )$$ with radius $$\frac{1}{2}$$ has upper semicircle $$y = \sqrt{x} \sqrt{1 - x} .$$ The sector of this circle bounded by the $$x$$ -axis between $$x = 0$$ and $$x = \frac{1}{2}$$ and by the line joining $$( \frac{1}{4} , \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} )$$ corresponds to $$\frac{1}{6}$$ of the circle and has area $$\frac{\pi}{24} .$$ This sector is the union of a right triangle with height $$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}$$ and base $$\frac{1}{4}$$ and the region below the graph between $$x = 0$$ and $$x = \frac{1}{4} .$$ To find the area of this region you can write $$y = \sqrt{x} \sqrt{1 - x} = \sqrt{x} \times ( \text{binomial expansion of} \sqrt{1 - x} )$$ and integrate term by term. Use this approach with the binomial approximation from the previous exercise to estimate $$\pi .$$

Problem Statement: The following exercises deal with Fresnel integrals .
Question: Use the approximation $$T \approx 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} ( 1 + \frac{k^{2}}{4} )$$ to approximate the period of a pendulum having length $$10$$ meters and maximum angle $$\theta_{\text{max}} = \frac{\pi}{6}$$ where $$k = \text{sin} ( \frac{\theta_{\text{max}}}{2} ) .$$ Compare this with the small angle estimate $$T \approx 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} .$$
Answer: $$T \approx 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{10}{9.8}} ( 1 + \frac{\text{sin}^{2} ( \theta \text{/} 12 )}{4} ) \approx 6.453$$ seconds. The small angle estimate is $$T \approx 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{10}{9.8} \approx 6.347} .$$ The relative error is around $$2$$ percent.

Problem Statement: The following exercises deal with Fresnel integrals .
Question: Suppose that a pendulum is to have a period of $$2$$ seconds and a maximum angle of $$\theta_{\text{max}} = \frac{\pi}{6} .$$ Use $$T \approx 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} ( 1 + \frac{k^{2}}{4} )$$ to approximate the desired length of the pendulum. What length is predicted by the small angle estimate $$T \approx 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} ?$$

Problem Statement: The following exercises deal with Fresnel integrals .
Question: Evaluate $$\int_{0}^{\pi \text{/} 2} \text{sin}^{4} \theta d \theta$$ in the approximation $$T = 4 \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} \int_{0}^{\pi \text{/} 2} ( 1 + \frac{1}{2} k^{2} \text{sin}^{2} \theta + \frac{3}{8} k^{4} \text{sin}^{4} \theta + \text{cdots} ) d \theta$$ to obtain an improved estimate for $$T .$$

Problem Statement: The following exercises deal with Fresnel integrals .
Question: [T] An equivalent formula for the period of a pendulum with amplitude $$\theta_{\text{max}}$$ is $$T ( \theta_{\text{max}} ) = 2 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} \int_{0}^{\theta_{\text{max}}} \frac{d \theta}{\sqrt{\text{cos} \theta} - \text{cos} ( \theta_{\text{max}} )}$$ where $$L$$ is the pendulum length and $$g$$ is the gravitational acceleration constant. When $$\theta_{\text{max}} = \frac{\pi}{3}$$ we get $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{\text{cos} t - 1 \text{/} 2}} \approx \sqrt{2} ( 1 + \frac{t^{2}}{2} + \frac{t^{4}}{3} + \frac{181 t^{6}}{720} ) .$$ Integrate this approximation to estimate $$T ( \frac{\pi}{3} )$$ in terms of $$L$$ and $$g .$$ Assuming $$g = 9.806$$ meters per second squared, find an approximate length $$L$$ such that $$T ( \frac{\pi}{3} ) = 2$$ seconds.
